name: GERD - PPI-Only Patients (No Diagnosis) — DOMINANT Path C
description: THE flagship Path C test case. PPIs are arguably the most cross-prescribed and over-prescribed drug class in primary care. Real-world reasons for a PPI prescription without a GERD diagnosis: dyspepsia (functional), NSAID-associated gastritis prophylaxis, H. pylori eradication regimen, prophylaxis in critically ill or anticoagulated patients, empirical relief of upper GI symptoms, even routine prescription with chronic anti-platelet therapy. Studies estimate 40-60% of PPI prescriptions lack a documented GI diagnosis. The Synthea module weights this path very heavily (~50%) to make this the most diagnostic Path C test in the suite.
prompt: I want to find patients in our system who are taking proton pump inhibitors but don't have a formal GERD diagnosis on their problem list. PPIs are commonly prescribed empirically for dyspepsia, NSAID gastritis prophylaxis, or other reasons without a formal reflux diagnosis being coded.

prompts.naive: Find patients on PPIs without a GERD diagnosis.
prompts.expert: I need to identify the dominant PPI-without-dx pattern. Two-step approach: first find patients with MedicationRequest for any PPI ingredient (RxNorm 7646 omeprazole, 40790 pantoprazole, 17128 lansoprazole, 283742 esomeprazole, 114979 rabeprazole), then exclude patients with a Condition for GERD-family codes (235595009, 698065002, 16331000). The remaining group represents PPI use without coded reflux — typically dyspepsia, NSAID prophylaxis, or empirical prescribing.

metadata.algorithm_path_description: Path C 'tricky' patients: have PPIs but NO GERD Condition resource. The Synthea module weights this path very heavily (~50%) — this is the largest Path C in the entire suite, reflecting real-world PPI over-prescribing for dyspepsia, NSAID prophylaxis, etc.
